title: El Salvador — ONUVA Children's Home
population: Orphaned children, trauma-affected youth, and school staff
summary: ONUVA Children's Home has been running daily neurofeedback sessions for over two years — 18,000+ sessions delivered, 97% of participants showing measurable improvement. This is our flagship deployment and our largest ongoing commitment, covering machines, consumables, training, and expansion as the program grows.
whatWeFund: Ongoing support for ONUVA — including the permanent purchase of a neurofeedback machine the school has been borrowing, supplies and consumables, practitioner training, and expansion to serve more children and staff as capacity allows.
whyThisPopulation:
The children at ONUVA are orphans or come from backgrounds of violence, poverty, and family separation. Many arrived with nervous systems locked in survival mode — unable to sleep, learn, or feel safe. The teachers and house parents who care for them carry their own unresolved trauma. What makes the ONUVA model powerful is that everyone receives sessions — children and adults alike. When the adults heal, the environment changes. When the environment changes, the children have a real chance.

Direct neurofeedback reaches what talk therapy cannot. It works at the neurological level, helping the brain regulate itself without requiring the child to verbalize or relive trauma. For kids who have never had the words — or the safety — to speak about what happened to them, this is what healing actually looks like.

title: 4 Calm Boxes for a Phoenix Group Home
summary: Funds four CalmWaves calm boxes for a group home in Phoenix serving foster care youth — giving residents and staff on-site access to direct neurofeedback for daily nervous system regulation.
whatWeFund: Four CalmWaves calm boxes ($300 each) deployed to a group home in Phoenix. Once funded, this project closes — additional gifts get directed to the next open project.
whyThisPopulation: Foster care youth carry compounded trauma — separation, instability, and often abuse. Most arrive at group homes already medicated, already in talk therapy, and still locked in fight-or-flight. Direct neurofeedback gives their nervous system a chance to settle, so the rest of the support they receive can actually take hold.
